coredata.dc:title: Switchable fluorescence immunoassay using gold nanoclusters anchored cobalt oxyhydroxide composite for sensitive detection of imidacloprid
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hongxia
Jin, Rui
Kong, Deshuai
Zhao, Xu
Liu, Fangmeng
Yan, Xu
Lin, Yuehe
Lu, Geyu
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of a convenient fluorescence-based immunoassay (FIA) for specific antigen attract increasing concern but remains a considerable challenge, especially the laborious synthesis of nanomaterials-antibody conjugates. Herein, we circumvent this drawback by introducing a specific fluorescence signal response procedure to commercially available alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-labeled conventional immunoassay for imidacloprid detection. In this FIA protocol, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) can anchor on the surface of two-dimensional cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes to form nanocomposite, resulting in remarkable decrease of fluorescence intensity. The quenching effects can be effectively reversed by introducing ascorbic acid that can trigger the decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes. Notably, the corresponding fluorescence response induced by ascorbic acid was related to ALP activities labeled on antibody. After competitive immunoreaction, the ALP-labeled antibodies were bounded to immobilized antigen, which can regulate the fluorescence change. Utilizing fluorescence switching of system, the 50% inhibition concentration (IC50) value of FIA toward imidacloprid was obtained at 1.3 ng mL−1 which was 60-fold-of-magnitude more sensitive than that of conventional ELISA (86.4 ng mL−1). This FIA protocol not only develops new prospects for pesticide detection, but also opens up potent strategy of fluorogenic immunoassay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Immunoassay
Fluorescence
Composite
Imidacloprid
Alkaline phosphatase

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of imidacloprid in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for fluorescence-based immunoassay of imidacloprid.

TEM image (A) and HRTEM image (B) of CoOOH nanoflakes. (C) XRD pattern of the CoOOH nanoflakes. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH nanoflakes and CoOOH + AuNCs. (E) FT-IR spectrum of CoOOH. (F) Zeta potential of AuNCs, CoOOH nanoflakes and CoOOH + AuNCs. (G) TEM image of CoOOH-AuNCs composite. Photostability (H) and salt stability (I) of CoOOH-AuNCs composite.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH nanoflakes (Red line) and the FL spectra of AuNCs (Black line). (B) The FL lifetime of AuNCs in the absence and presence of CoOOH nanoflakes. (C) The FL spectra of AuNCs in the presence of various concentrations of CoOOH nanoflakes (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20 and 30 μg mL−1). Inset was the FL quenching efficiency of CoOOH. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNCs in the presence of different concentration of CoOOH nanoflakes (from 0 to 20 μg mL-1). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Schematic representation of the ALP strategy based on CoOOH-AuNCs composite. (B) Feasibility of sensor. FL spectra of AuNCs, CoOOH-AuNCs, CoOOH-AuNCs + AA and CoOOH-AuNCs + AAP + ALP. (C) The FL spectra of CoOOH-AuNCs + AAP system in the presence of various concentrations of ALP (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 50, 100, 500 and 1000 U L−1). Inset was FL intensity trends of system in the presence of ALP. (D) The linear plot of FALP/FALP0 versus the logarithm concentration of ALP. (E) The FL intensity of the CoOOH-AuNCs system and the CoOOH-AuNCs-ALP system with the interfering substances (100 μg mL-1 for protein and 1000 μg mL−1 for others).

(A) Schematic representation of FIA. (B) The FL spectra of the FIA toward imidacloprid standards with various concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10 and 50 ng mL−1). (C) The linear plot of IE (%) versus the concentration of imidacloprid. (D) FL responses of FIA against imidacloprid or other control pesticide (5 ng mL−1). (E) FL responses of FIA against imidacloprid or other control biological species (100 μg mL−1 for protein and 1000 μg mL−1 for others).
Detection of imidacloprid in real samples.

head.ce:title: Switchable fluorescence immunoassay using gold nanoclusters anchored cobalt oxyhydroxide composite for sensitive detection of imidacloprid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University, Pullman, WA 99164, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of a convenient fluorescence-based immunoassay (FIA) for specific antigen attract increasing concern but remains a considerable challenge, especially the laborious synthesis of nanomaterials-antibody conjugates. Herein, we circumvent this drawback by introducing a specific fluorescence signal response procedure to commercially available alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-labeled conventional immunoassay for imidacloprid detection. In this FIA protocol, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) can anchor on the surface of two-dimensional cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes to form nanocomposite, resulting in remarkable decrease of fluorescence intensity. The quenching effects can be effectively reversed by introducing ascorbic acid that can trigger the decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes. Notably, the corresponding fluorescence response induced by ascorbic acid was related to ALP activities labeled on antibody. After competitive immunoreaction, the ALP-labeled antibodies were bounded to immobilized antigen, which can regulate the fluorescence change. Utilizing fluorescence switching of system, the 50% inhibition concentration (IC50) value of FIA toward imidacloprid was obtained at 1.3 ng mL−1 which was 60-fold-of-magnitude more sensitive than that of conventional ELISA (86.4 ng mL−1). This FIA protocol not only develops new prospects for pesticide detection, but also opens up potent strategy of fluorogenic immunoassay.

ce:section.ce:para: Imidacloprid, as an efficient systemic insecticide, has been extensively utilized to prevent, control, or eliminate pests on crops, highly boosting the productivity in modern agriculture. However, excessive use and improper use of pesticide also cause widespread contamination of natural water, soil, air, and farm products, even severely breakdown ecosystems [1,2]. More importantly, imidacloprid may damage the DNA of human peripheral lymphocytes even at trace levels [3], causing a huge danger to human health. In this regard, the sensitive analysis of pesticide residues in food and environmental matrixes is of great significance to satisfy the demand for food security, to protect the ecosystem and to safeguard human health.

The most popular assay type, specific antibody-antigen recognition-based immunoassay has emerged as a promising strategy to achieve pesticide detection due to its operational simplicity, high specificity and remarkable selectivity, as well as high-throughput [4–6]. Owing to the merit of easy-to-observe and facility-to-use, colorimetric based signal output strategy turns out to be the most commonly developed approach for immunoassay, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7–9] and lateral flow immunochromatographic strip [10,11]. For example, Hammock’s group developed a competitive ELISA for the analysis of imidacloprid with low cross-reactivity by using a horseradish peroxidase modified polyclonal antibody [12]. Inspired by this work, horseradish peroxidase-labeled hapten [13] and gold nanoparticles-mediated monoclonal antibody [14] were also synthesized as transducers in amplifying the response signals for screening imidacloprid. However, one main drawback of traditional colorimetric ELISA using enzyme or nanomaterials as labels is its poor sensitivity when monitoring low-abundant target in complex environmental and food matrix. For example, sample dilution is necessary before pesticide analysis to reduce the background effect. Once pesticide is diluted, the detection sensitivity of ELISA cannot meet the monitoring requirements. Furthermore, these established approaches are also subjected to the limitations of reaction substrate with toxicity, such as o-phenylenediamine and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine.

To address these issues, considerable efforts have been made in exploring new output signal mode of antigen-antibody binding event including fluorescence [15], chemiluminescence [16,17] and electrochemistry [18]. Among those various strategies, fluorescence-based immunoassay (FIA) as a promising candidate have sparked significant investigative excitement due to the emergence of exceptional fluorescent materials with outstanding optical characteristic [19,20]. In fact, the great majority of current FIAs concentrate on taking advantage of fluorescent materials as label to produce detectable signals instead of marker enzyme [21–24]. Though impactful improvement in sensitivity have been obtained, most FIAs in this category required the complex signal response procedures and mechanisms including fluorescence anisotropy/polarization measurements, nanomaterials-antibody conjugation and immunoagglutination. Hence, how to simplify experiment procedure and also improve performance of sensing are playing an important role to FIAs. Recently, by combining enzyme (alkaline phosphatase, ALP)-based immunoassay with fluorescent platform, a series of FIA protocol are developed, which employ marker enzyme to trigger the fluorescence (FL) change of nanomaterials [25–28]. Those strategies not only effectively improve the sensing sensitivity and shield the toxicity of substrate, but also greatly simplify the modification and conjugation procedure. However, the above-mentioned research was restricted to use only one nanomaterial. Naturally, we believe that the combinations of fluorophore and nanomaterial-based quencher in a FIA system would possess promising performance. In light of this, a new inspired FIA protocol was designed by using gold nanoclusters (AuNCs)-anchored cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanocomposite for the sensitive monitoring of a pesticide: imidacloprid.

As shown in Scheme 1 , it is simple to prepare CoOOH-AuNCs composite, whereas CoOOH nanoflakes as quencher can efficiently suppress the FL intensity of AuNCs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. By employing the specific redox reaction with ascorbic acid (AA), the CoOOH nanoflakes were decomposed to generate Co2+, accompanying the subsequent recovery of FL of platform. On the basis of AA-stimulated FL responds, the sensing platform can specially recognize ALP that enzymatic conversion of l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to form AA. Notably, the convincing ALP-participated catalysis procedure was introduced to the conventional ELISA for fabricating FIA strategy, in which ALP were employed as the marker enzyme to produce detectable signals, making it feasible for quantitatively detecting target antigen in a FL enhancement manner. This new FIA protocol is sensitive and selective for pesticide detection by taking advantage of the antigen-antibody specific recognition, the specificity of enzymatic reaction, and the sensitivity of CoOOH-AuNCs composite probe.

Hexagonal CoOOH nanoflakes were firstly prepared by using NaClO to readily oxidize CoCl2 in the presence of KOH. The CoOOH nanoflake was composed of an edge-shared CoO6 octahedral that possess the thickness on the order of nanometers. The product was identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diﬀ ;raction (XRD), UV–vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 1 ). The TEM image in Fig. 1A indicated that the morphology of CoOOH nanoflakes exhibited uniform hexagonal plate shape with average diameters nearly 75 nm. To understand the subtle structure of nanomaterials, Fig. 1B displayed the HRTEM image of CoOOH nanoflakes, recording that a lattice spacing of nanoflake is around 0.229 nm, which matched well with the d-spacing of (012) plane of CoOOH nanoflakes [29]. The XRD patterns (Fig. 1C) illustrated that the as-synthesis CoOOH nanoflakes exist in hexagonal rhomb-centered phase (JCPDS no.07-0169), which was same as literature report [30]. Subsequently, CoOOH nanoshets were characterized by UV–vis absorption, as depict in Fig. 1D, CoOOH nanoflakes expressed a strong wide-band absorption from 350 to 700 nm with a characteristic absorption centered at 413 nm, which made it an efficient quencher to a wide variety of FL donors, including the AuNCs utilized in this system. Compared to absorption spectrum of CoCl2 (peak at 510 nm), an obvious blue-shifted of CoOOH absorption was found (Figure S1), further confirming the formation of CoOOH nanoflakes. Furthermore, the composition and surface structure of nanoflakes were explored. The zeta potential of CoOOH nanoflakes were 8.7 mV, implying a positive charge on flake surfaces. Fig. 1E illustrated the FT-IR spectrum of nanomaterial in the range from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1. The broad band of CoOOH centered at about 3300 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of the hydrogen-bonded hydroxyl group (−OH). The peak located around 1652 cm−1 was correspond to the Co-O double bond in the crystal structure of CoOOH. In addition, the peak of Co-O2- complex vibration can be observed at 579 cm−1. Interestingly, the CoOOH nanoflakes possessed excellent colloid stability in water for 120 min without any agglomeration (Figure S2). These results proved that CoOOH nanoflakes have been successfully synthesized with good stability, making them promise for construction composite. AuNCs were facilely prepared through a one-pot synthetic method by employing GSH as template and stabilizer [31] (Figure S3).

The CoOOH-AuNCs composite that consists of CoOOH nanoflakes and AuNCs were easily fabricated through sonicate. The CoOOH nanoflakes were positively charged (ζ = 8.7) while AuNCs were negatively charged (ζ = −25.2) (Fig. 1F), demonstrating intense electrostatic interaction etween nanoflakes and AuNCs. After introducing AuNCs, the zeta potential was changed to be -17.8. And the composite exhibited an absorption band centered at 408 nm, which possess a blue-shift of 5 nm compared to that of CoOOH nanoflakes (Fig. 1D), which caused by the electrostatic interaction. To confirm the formation of nanocomposite, the CoOOH-AuNCs composite was successfully observed by TEM image (Fig. 1G), clearly displaying that the AuNCs were well interspersed on the surface of COOOH nanoflakes. We then systematically investigated chemical stability and photostability of CoOOH-AuNCs composite. As depict in Fig. 1H and I, the FL emission intensity of probe exhibited high stability in the presence of high concentration of NaCl (10 mmol L−1) and 60-min irradiation under 150 W xenon lamp, making them suitable for sensing ALP.

In CoOOH-AuNCs nanocomposite, CoOOH nanoflakes were served as quencher that can efficiently suppress the FL intensity of AuNCs. To prove the FL quenching mechanism of AuNCs, the optical properties of the independent nanomaterials and the composite were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 2 A, the absorption band of CoOOH nanoflakes (Red line) significantly overlapped with the emissions of the AuNCs (Black line) from 550 to 630 nm. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 2B, the lifetime of AuNCs were obviously shortened after introducing CoOOH nanoflakes (10 μg mL−1), which implied that the quenching sensing mechanism was attributed to FRET process. To further investigate the FRET between CoOOH and AuNCs, the FL emission intensity of probe was collected by adding diﬀ ;erent concentrations of CoOOH nanoflakes. As expected, with the increase of CoOOH nanoflakes concentration from 0 to 30 μg mL−1, the FL intensity of AuNCs gradually decreased in Fig. 2C, which is accompanied by remarkable increase of absorbance intensity (Fig. 2D). It can be obviously seen that the FL quenching efficiency can reach 90% after incubation with 30 μg mL−1 CoOOH nanoflakes (inset Fig. 2C), which mainly assigned to the stronger optical absorption and larger surface area of CoOOH nanoflakes.

Having successfully synthesized the CoOOH-AuNCs composite, the possibility of AA detection using the hybrid materials was investigated. After adding AA to CoOOH-AuNCs composite system, CoOOH nanoflakes can be effectively reduced to generate Co2+ because of the redox reactions between CoOOH nanoflakes and AA, resulting in the decomposition of nanocomposite and the FL recover of probe (Blue line, Fig. 3 B). As shown in equation (1), AA with enediol group was oxidized to produce dehydroascorbic acid [32].

The decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes to produce Co2+ was first verified by UV–vis absorption spectra (Figure S4). Meanwhile, AA and Co2+ did not cause notable FL change of AuNCs, ensuring the AA and Co2+ had no distinct interference on system (Figure S5 and Figure S6). After that, to obtain optimal performance for AA analysis, the work concentration of CoOOH nanoflakes and reaction time that directly influence the detection sensitivity and background signal were explored in Figure S7. Under the optimum conditions, the performance of CoOOH-AuNCs composite for AA response was studied. Figure S8 showed the FL intensity of CoOOH-AuNCs system at 575 nm was gradually enhanced with increasing AA concentration, indicating that the correlation between recovered FL intensity and AA concentration was dose-dependent. Thus, bestowed with good analytical performance, the CoOOH-AuNCs platform can be applied for monitoring of ALP-induced biocatalytic reaction.

Fig. 3A illustrates the strategy of CoOOH-AuNCs system for ALP monitoring. The basic principle of biosensor includes enzyme-induced AAP hydrolysis and AA-caused the FL recover of CoOOH-AuNCs [33,34]. As the content of AA in this system was controlled by ALP, the corresponding FL recovery of CoOOH-AuNCs probe offer a quantitative output signal for enzyme activity (Green line, Fig. 3B). It can be seen from Figure S9 and S10 that AAP (10–150 μmol L−1) and ALP (10–1000 U L−1) cannot induce obviously changes of CoOOH-AuNCs probe, demonstrating the interaction between AAP/ALP and CoOOH-AuNCs can be ignored. For better sensing performances, many factors including pH, incubation temperature and reaction time were optimized for the determination of ALP (data shown in Supporting information, Figure S11). After that, the CoOOH-AuNCs nanocomposite were mixed with 2.0 mmol L−1 of AAP and different concentrations of ALP (from 0 to 1000 U L−1) for 50 min at 37 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the FL intensity of CoOOH-AuNCs probe was gradually restored with the concentration of ALP increasing. Meanwhile, an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9939) preformed between the FL ratio (FALP/FALP0, where FALP and FALP0 refer to the FL intensity in the presence and in the absence of ALP) and logarithm concentration of ALP, ranging from 0.1 to 1000 U L−1. The regression equation was FALP/FALP0 = 1.2499 + 0.2184 Log [ALP], U L−1 (Fig. 3D). The detection limit of ALP was calculated to be 0.05 U L−1, which was much lower or comparable with previous strategies reported by literature (Table S1). The influence of some common substances in biological samples, such as ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Mg2+), amino acids (arginine, glycine, tyrosine, aspartate, threonine, phenylalanine), glucose, GSH and protein (BSA, trypsin, HRP, GoX) were investigated (Fig. 4 C). In marked contrast, only the presence of 10 μg mL−1 ALP (500 U L−1) induced a prominent FL change, whereas very weak FL variations of system were observed upon the addition of excess of these substances (10-fold for protein and 100-fold for others). In addition, even in the presence of these coexistence substance, the established system was unaffected for the determination of ALP activity (Fig. 4D), indicating that the CoOOH-AuNCs platform performed high selectivity and well ability of resisting interference. In previous studies, manganese dioxide nanosheets [34], noble metal nanoparticles [35] and metal ion [36,37] were employed as quencher for the fabrication of ALP sensors, which can be easily influenced by GSH on account of reducing activity and special affinity with metal of GSH. Notably, in comparison with previous nanosensors, GSH cannot cause obvious interference to CoOOH-based ALP probe. The high selective property of the probe can be ascribed to two efficient processes: one is the specific redox reaction between CoOOH nanoflakes and AA. The enediol group as the reductive unit of AA can specifically react with CoOOH to yield Co2+. The other is the distinctive catalytic ability of ALP, carrying out characteristic enzymatic hydrolysis ability to AAP under alkaline conditions. Therefore, the established CoOOH-AuNCs nanosystem was sensitive enough for selective detection of ALP, showing promising potential to be extended into enzyme-regulated FIA for pesticide.

Inspired by the successful application of ALP as a biocatalytic label in immunoassays [25,38–40], ALP activity sensing system with good performance possess a great potential to be applied in FIA for target antigen. As a proof of concept, we utilized ALP-labeled antibodies as signal tag. CoOOH-AuNCs system was exploited as fluorogenic substrate to replace the conventional colorimetric substrate. Fig. 4A illustrates the integration of competition-type immunorecognition of antigen/antibody pair and ALP-mediated FL responds of CoOOH-AuNCs system for the detection of imidacloprid. The procedures of the proposed FIA are similar to that of conventional ELISA. Initially, the microtiter plates were modified with coating antigen, and then blocked with BSA solution. In the presence of imidacloprid, coating antigen and pesticide were competed to capture anti-imidacloprid antibody. On account of the different pesticide concentration, the formed coating antigen-antibody complex in 96-well plate were closely associated with the target. After bound with ALP-labelled detection antibody to form a signal path, CoOOH-AuNCs/AAP solution as fluorogenic substrate was introduced for signal amplification and indicator. Thus, the relationship between concentration of imidacloprid and FL intensity of probe exhibit inverse proportion. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the FL emission intensity of the reaction solution at 575 nm exhibited a gradual decrease as the imidacloprid concentration increased. Specifically, in the imidacloprid concentration range from 0.1 to 50 ng mL−1, the inhibition efficiency (IE%) vary linearly with imidacloprid levels (R2 = 0.990), with the estimated IC50 value (half-maximal inhibitory concentration) of 1.3 ng mL−1 (Fig. 4C). The imidacloprid detection limit (LOD) was found to be 0.1 ng mL−1, which was better or comparable to reported probe (Table S2). More importantly, the performance of FIA fulfilled the analysis requirement in food samples (the maximum residue limit of imidacloprid was in the range of 50–1000 ng mL−1), which set by Ministry of Agriculture in China (GB 2763-2014).

We further evaluated the sensitivity between established FIA with traditional ELISA by testing imidacloprid in the range from 0.1 to 1000 ng mL−1 using the two protocols. As revealed in Figure S12, the IC50 of traditional ELISA were calculated to be 86.4 ng mL−1. Taken together, the sensitivity of the ALP-labeled FIA was 60-fold-of-magnitude more sensitive than that of conventional ELISA. Such extraordinarily high sensitivity was arised from the following distinctive features: (i) different from colorimetric ELISA, CoOOH-AuNCs/AAP/ALP based system possess FL responds to imidacloprid, providing switchable FL signal in high sensitivity; (ii) the fluorogenic substrate (CoOOH-AuNCs system) performed higher structural stability and lower toxicity compared with the colorimetric substrate (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) [41]; (iii) the combination of CoOOH nanoflakes and AuNCs that made low background signal can be considerably conducive to improving the sensitivity for quantitative detection of target [42]. Overall, this strategy provided a new pathway for achieving pesticide analysis by FIA protocols.

Selective recognition ability is an important indicator to examine the performance of established system, especially for enzyme-based immunoassay in real-time applications [43]. To better simulate the reaction environment, the influence of some common pesticide, such as organophosphorus pesticide (parathion, paraoxon, chlorpyrifos, malathion), neonicotinoid pesticide (thiacloprid, thiamethoxam, tinotefuran, nitenpyram), pyrethroid pesticide (cypermethrin) and carbamate pesticide (metolcarb, carbaryl, isoprocorb) were investigated as the controls. The FL results in Fig. 4D unambiguously illustrate that only imidacloprid (5 ng mL−1) caused remarkable change, indicating that this system is highly selective to imidacloprid. What’s more, in practical applications, the performance of the FIA protocol in the presence of food and environment matrix was further measured. The influences of some common electrolytes and biological species including ions, amino acids, glucose, GSH and protein was investigated in Fig. 4E. After introducing the above substances (100 μg mL−1 for protein and 1000 μg mL−1 for others), the FL intensity of the FIA system remained nearly constant (change less than 10%), indicating that the common electrolytes and biological species also displayed negative influence on imidacloprid detection. Thus, the FIA system not only processed great potential for monitoring imidacloprid in agricultural and environmental samples but also performed huge promise for evaluating imidacloprid poisoning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of imidacloprid in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate its practicality, the as-developed FIA platform was used to detect imidacloprid in agricultural and food sample via the standard addition method. In this case, the samples (tap water, river water, soil, rice and apple) were spiked at 50, 500 and 1000 ng mL−1 with imidacloprid standard. Then, the samples were diluted 50-fold with PBS buffer (pH = 6.5) to resist background interference and monitored by using the developed FIA protocol. As displayed in Table 1 , recoveries of the known spiked amounts of imidacloprid in real samples were obtained in the range of 85.4–107.4 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 5.5%. And the obtained results showed acceptable agreement with that of gas chromatography (GC), suggesting the potential applicability of the proposed strategy for imidacloprid detection in real samples.

In summary, by taking advantage of ALP-triggered substrate hydrolysis as well as the AA-controlled decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes, we have designed CoOOH-AuNCs composite to fabricate ALP labeled-FIA protocol for quantitative analysis of antigen. Choosing imidacloprid as the model analyte, the established FIA can determine the analyte down to 0.1 ng mL−1 by using FL spectroscopy. The integration of specific antibody-antigen recognition, efficient enzymatic reaction and excellent optical characteristic of CoOOH-AuNCs composite considerably contribute to the sensitivity improvement. Meanwhile, the versatile and modular platform not only effectively shield the toxicity of substrate, but also greatly eliminate the requirement of modification and conjugation procedure. Accordingly, this new FIA protocol may increase scientific understanding and technological developments for constructing general and potent strategy of fluorogenic immunoassay in bioassay applications, making it practically useful for environment monitoring, food analysis, and clinical diagnosis.

The water with good resistivity (> 18 MΩ cm−1) were utilized in this work. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate (HAuCl4• xH2O), l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) and cobaltous chloride (CoCl2·6H2O), l-glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA) and amino acids of analytical grade were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. Sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium hydroxide (KOH), BSA (bovine serum albumin), trypsin, HRP (Horseradish Peroxidase), GoX (glucoseoxidase) and imidacloprid were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology CO. LTD.. Anti-imidacloprid Antibody was prepared by our laboratory (See Supporting Information). RF-5301 PC spectroﬂuorophotometer (Japan) and Shimadzu UV-1700 UV–vis spectrophotometer was employed to test the FL spectra and UV–vis spectra.

1.0 mol L−1 of KOH (125 μL) and 10 mol L−1 of CoCl2 (500 μL) were mixed and sonicated for 1 min. Subsequently, 0.9 mol L−1 of NaClO (25 μL) was added to the above solution, and the mixture was sonicated for another 10 min. The product was collected by centrifugation for 10 min at 10,000 rpm, then washed with deionized water for three times. Finally, CoOOH nanoflakes were freeze dried to a brownish black powder.

GSH capped AuNCs were synthesized based on the previous process with minor modification [31,44]. In brief, 0.5 mL of HAuCl4 (20 mmol L−1) and 0.15 mL of GSH (100 mmol L−1) were mixed and added to 4.35 mL of ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. Then, the above mixture was heated to 70 °C and reaction was allowed to proceed for 24 h. Thus, a yellow solution of AuNCs was obtained and purified by use of dialysis bag (1 kDa). Finally, AuNCs were freeze dried to a yellow powder.

5.0 mL of as-synthesized AuNCs solution (0.20 mg mL−1) were added to 5.0 mL of the CoOOH solution (0.25 mg mL−1), then the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. After that, the mixed solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm, then the precipitation was dissolved with 10 mL deionized water to obtain CoOOH-AuNCs composite solution (0.225 mg mL−1).

Different concentrations of ALP (100 μL) and 2.0 mmol L−1 of AAP (100 μL) were mixed at 37 °C for 45 min. Then, CoOOH-AuNCs composite (100 μL) and 10 mmol L−1 of Tris−HCl buffer (pH = 9.0, 100 μL) were added and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. Next, 1600 μL of ultrapure water were added and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

200 μL per well of coating antigens (200 ng mL−1) was added to the 96-well plate, the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The plate was blocked with 300 μL of bovine serum albumin (BSA, 5.0 mg mL−1) for 60 min at 37 °C. Then, different concentration imidacloprid (50 μL) and anti-imidacloprid polyclonal antibodies (50 μL) were added to the plate. After incubating at 37 °C for 60 min, ALP-labeled secondary antibodies were added at 37 °C for another 60 min. For the above procedure, the plates were washed three times with Tween-20 (PBST). Subsequently, 2.0 mmol L−1 of AAP (100 μL) were added at 37 °C for 60 min. The above reaction solution was mixed with CoOOH-AuNCs composite (100 μL) and 10 mmol L−1 of Tris−HCl buffer (pH = 9.0, 100 μL) for another 10 min at 37 °C. Next, 1700 μL of ultrapure water were added and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFC0207300), National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61831011, 61503148, 61520106003 and 21806051), Science and Technology Development Program of Jilin Province (No. 2017052016JH). Program for JLU Science and Technology Innovative Research Team (JLUSTIRT 2017TD-07). Scientific and Technological “13th Five-Year Plan” Project of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (JJKH20190111KJ). The Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities. China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project(No. 2018M631862 and 2017M621199). X. Yan is thankful for support from the National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX201700096).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.026.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials for sensing and destroying pesticides
Review of optical sensors for pesticides
Development of an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for the simultaneous determination of parathion and imidacloprid
Immunochemical techniques for multianalyte analysis of chemical residues in food and the environment: a review
Bioinspired synthesis of all-in-one organic-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers combined with a handheld pH meter for on-site detection of food pathogen
Self-assembly of ferritin nanoparticles into an enzyme nanocomposite with tunable size for ultrasensitive immunoassay
Horseradish peroxidase-mediated, iodide-catalyzed cascade reaction for plasmonic immunoassays
Membrane-based lateral flow immunochromatographic strip with nanoparticles as reporters for detection: a review
Two-color lateral flow assay for multiplex detection of causative agents behind acute febrile illnesses
Development of an ELISA for the detection of the residues of the insecticide imidacloprid in agricultural and environmental samples
Development of competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) based on monoclonal antibodies for chloronicotinoid insecticides imidacloprid and acetamiprid
Strip-based immunoassay for the simultaneous detection of the neonicotinoid insecticides imidacloprid and thiamethoxam in agricultural products
Fluorescence polarisation immunoassays for strobilurin fungicides kresoxim-methyl, trifloxystrobin and picoxystrobin
Colorimetric and chemiluminescent dual-readout immunochromatographic assay for detection of pesticide residues utilizing g-C3N4/BiFeO3 nanocomposites
Dual-readout immunochromatographic assay by utilizing MnO2 nanoflowers as the unique colorimetric/ chemiluminescent probe
A novel nanoparticle-based disposable electrochemical immunosensor for diagnosis of exposure to toxic organophosphorus agents
An enzyme cascade-triggered fluorogenic and chromogenic reaction applied in enzyme activity assay and immunoassay
Multiplexible wash-free immunoassay using colloidal assemblies of magnetic and photoluminescent nanoparticles
Sensitive rapid fluorescence polarization immunoassay for free mycophenolic acid determination in human serum and plasma
Mobile phone ratiometric imaging enables highly sensitive fluorescence lateral flow immunoassays without external optical filters
Brilliant pitaya-type silica colloids with central-radial and high-density quantum dots incorporation for ultrasensitive fluorescence immunoassays
A universal multi-wavelength fluorescence polarization immunoassay for multiplexed detection of mycotoxins in maize
Fluorescence immunoassay system via enzyme-enabled in situ synthesis of fluorescent silicon nanoparticles
Peptidic beta-sheet binding with Congo Red allows both reduction of error variance and signal amplification for immunoassays
Redox-mediated indirect fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of disease biomarkers using dopamine-functionalized quantum dots
Switchable fluorescence of gold nanoclusters for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and its application in immunoassay
CoOOH nanosheets on cobalt substrate as a non-enzymatic glucose sensor
Chemical redox modulated fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase
From aggregation-induced emission of Au(I)-thiolate complexes to ultrabright Au(0)@Au(I)-thiolate core-shell nanoclusters
Hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide-carbon dots hybridized surface: high sensitive fluorescence turn-on probe for monitoring of ascorbic acid in rat brain following brain ischemia
Self-assembled gold nanoclusters for fluorescence turn-on and colorimetric dual-readout detection of alkaline phosphatase activity via DCIP-mediated fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets

Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity in L-02 cells and its application in living cell imaging based on in-situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots
Stimulus response of Au-NPs@GMP-Tb core-shell nanoparticles: Toward colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity in algal blooms of a freshwater lake
Copper-mediated DNA-scaffolded silver nanocluster on-off switch for detection of pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase
Hydroquinone diphosphate as a phosphatase substrate in enzymatic amplification combined with electrochemical-chemical-chemical redox cycling for the detection of E. coli O157:H7
Versatile and amplified biosensing through enzymatic cascade reaction by coupling alkaline phosphatase in situ generation of photoresponsive nanozyme
Photoinduced electron transfer between Fe(III) and adenosine triphosphate-BODIPY conjugates: Application to alkaline-phosphatase-linked immunoassay
Highly selective fluorogenic multianalyte biosensors constructed via enzyme-catalyzed coupling and aggregation-induced emission
Label-free fluorescent assay for high sensitivity and selectivity detection of acid phosphatase and inhibitor screening
Ultrasensitive glutathione detection based on lucigenin cathodic electrochemiluminescence in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets
One-pot green synthesis of supramolecular ꞵ-cyclodextrin functionalized gold nanoclusters and their application for highly selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of dopamine

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongxia Li received her M.S. degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. and received her pH.D. degree in 2016 at Jilin University. Since then, she did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu. Currently, her research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Rui Jin received her M.S. degree in 2016 from College of Chemistry, Jilin University, China. Currently, she is studying for her pH.D. degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University. Her research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Deshuai Kong received the B. Eng. degree in department of Microelectronics science and engineering, Jilin University in 2018. Now, she is about to assiduously study for her MS degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Xu Zhao received the B.Eng.degree in department of electronic sciences and technology,Jilin University in 2016.Now,he is studying for his MS degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering,Jilin University,China. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Fangmeng Liu received his PhD degree in 2017 from College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China. Now he is a lecturer of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the application of functional materials and development of solid state electrolyte gas sensor and flexible device.

Xu Yan received his M.S. degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. He joined the group of Prof. Xingguang Su at Jilin University and received his pH.D. degree in June 2017. Since then, he did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu and Prof. Junqiu Liu. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Yuehe Lin is a professor at Washington State University and a Laboratory Fellow at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. His research interests include electrochemistry, bioanalytical chemistry, chemical sensors and biosensors, fuel cells and batteries, material synthesis and applications. He has over 10 patents and has published about 400 papers, with total citation ∼35,000, H-index 94. He is a fellow of the American Association of the Advancement of Science (AAAS), the Royal Society of Chemistry of the UK, and the American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering (AIMBE).

Geyu Lu received the B. Sci. degree in electronic sciences in 1985 and the M. Sci. degree in 1988 from Jilin University in China and the Dr. Eng. degree in 1998 from Kyushu University in Japan. Now he is a professor of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and the application of the function materials.
